definition: The total concentration of acids in wine, primarily tartaric acid (stable, grape-derived), malic acid (tart, grape-derived, convertible via MLF), citric acid (minor, grape-derived), and lactic acid (softer, produced during malolactic conversion). Acidity is perceived as sourness on the palate and is expressed in the SAT as low / medium(-) / medium / medium(+) / high.
distinction_insights: Acidity is not a single compound — tartaric and malic acid behave differently and can be selectively manipulated (MLF converts malic to lactic; tartaric is unaffected by MLF).
High acidity does not equal high quality; balance with fruit, alcohol, and residual sugar determines whether acidity is a positive structural component.
In cool climates, higher malic acid at harvest means MLF has a proportionally larger effect on perceived acidity reduction than in warm-climate wines.
Acidity affects ageability by protecting wine from oxidation (lower pH) and providing structural longevity.
